The course will also help students develop their skills in oral and written scientific communication.

title: Experiential Learning II
description: A learning opportunity (e.g., a research project, internship, or outreach activity) that is customized by the student to be relevant to their specific interests.

title: Fundamentals of Dynamic Meteorology
description: Introduction into dynamic meteorology, including the primitive equations, scale analysis, rotational effects, boundary layers, quasi-geostrophy, and atmospheric waves..

title: Fundamentals of Physical Meteorology
description: Overview of fundamental physical processes in the atmospheric sciences. Topics include thermodynamics, radiative transfer, and cloud microphysics.

title: Climate Dynamics
description: Analysis of the Earth climate system consisting of the atmosphere, the oceans, and the land surfaces. Topics include the global energy balance, the hydrological cycle, the general circulation, atmospheric radiative transfer, and natural and anthropogenic climate change.

title: Environmental Statistics
description: Numerical techniques used in atmospheric modeling. Statistical methods in environmental sciences including time series analysis, multivariate data analysis, statistical forecasting, forecast verification, and hypothesis testing.

title: Environmental Instrumentation
description: Understanding our environment requires understanding how measurements of it are made.  The techniques and instrumentation required to observe conditions near and immediately above the earth's surface are introduced.  Laboratory and field exercises are used to develop experience applicable for careers in the atmospheric and environmental fields.

title: Introduction to Atmospheric Chemistry & Air Pollution
description: The course will apply basic principles of physics and chemistry to quantitatively describe the processes that control the chemical composition and evolution of the Earth's atmosphere. Special topics include acid rain, the ozone hole, photochemical smog production, health effects of air pollution, and alternative energy sources.

title: Synoptic-Dynamic Meteorology I
description: A survey of modern-day synoptic meteorology and its applications, with emphasis on the integrated use of numerical analysis, atmospheric dynamics and physical meteorology to understand, explain, and predict the evolution of mid-latitude weather systems.

title: Weather Discussion
description: Student-led analysis and prediction of current and future weather using modern analysis and forecast tools. Repetitive for up to 2 credit hours.

title: Cloud System Modeling
description: Numerical modeling of turbulent, convective, and mesoscale motions associated with cloud systems. Formulation of physical processes in cloud-resolving models. Role of modeling efforts in understanding the structure and behavior of cloud systems. Representation of clouds and cloud processes in numerical weather prediction and climate models.

title: Atmospheric Radiation
description: Theory of solar and infrared radiation; fundamentals of energy balance and climate models; parameterization of infrared and solar-flux transfer in clear, aerosol, and cloudy atmospheres; climate perturbations due to greenhouse gases, aerosols and clouds.

title: Synoptic-Dynamic Meteorology II
description: Characteristics of surface-based fronts, upper-level fronts, and mid-latitude cyclones, including the use of quasigeostrophic theory and potential vorticity thinking for synoptic analysis.

title: Boundary Layer Meteorology
description: Boundary layer characteristics; Reynolds averaging; equations for turbulent flow; turbulence kinetic energy, stability, and scaling; turbulence closure; boundary conditions; convective mixed layer; stable boundary layer; cloud-topped boundary layer; boundary layer modeling.

title: Mesoscale Meteorology
description: Quantitative description of cumulus convection, multicell and supercell storms, tropical cyclones, local circulations (thermal and terrain forcing), and downslope windstorms. Whenever possible, we will undertake real-time examination of storms as the may develop around the country. Emphasis is on using observed characteristics to develop a physical and dynamical understanding of phenomena over a range of scales. Students will undertake evaluation of recent literature or an independent research paper.

title: Land-Atmosphere Interactions
description: The land and atmosphere interact in different ways, at various timescales, to affect the climate, weather, and air quality. This course will examine how the atmosphere impacts fluxes of water, carbon, and other pollutants and, in turn, how these fluxes affect the atmosphere. The course combines lectures, computer labs, and perusal of the primary literature to arrive at a multi-faceted understanding of land-atmosphere interactions.

title: Mountain Meteorology
description: Synoptic and mesoscale meteorology in complex terrain including orographically-modified cyclone evolution, frontal interaction with topography, terrain- and thermally-driven circulations, mountain waves, downslope winds, gap winds, and orographic precipitation.

title: Advanced Cloud Physics
description: Survey of small scale and large scale processes in the tropics. We will place special emphasis on near-real time examination of any tropical cyclones and hurricanes that may develop during the early weeks of the course, including operational and research datasets. We shall also include quantitative description of tropical phenomena including El Nino-Southern Oscillation, Madden-Julian Oscillation, easterly waves, and interactions between convective, mesoscale, and large-scale circulations, applications of remote sensing, and field programs. Emphasis is on using observed characteristics to develop a physical and dynamical understanding of phenomena over a range of scales. Students will undertake evaluation of recent literature or an independent research paper.

title: Wildfire Behavior, Weather, and Ecology
description: This interdisciplinary course explores the complex interactions between wildfire behavior, atmospheric conditions, and ecological systems. Students delve into the science behind wildfire dynamics, including ignition, spread, and intensity, while examining the role of weather patterns and climate variability. The course addresses the ecological impacts of wildfire, from ecosystem resilience and regeneration to the consequences for biodiversity and carbon cycling. Through a combination of lectures, case studies, and hands-on activities, students gain a comprehensive understanding of the multifaceted nature of wildfire science and its critical importance in the context of climate change.

title: Numerical Weather Prediction
description: Comprehensive introduction to modern numerical weather forecasting theory and techniques, concentrating on concepts and advanced methods of atmospheric modeling, data assimilation, and predictability.

title: Tropical Meteorology
description: Survey of small scale and large scale processes in the tropics. Special topics such as tropical cyclones, ENSO, interactions between convective, mesoscale, and large-scale circulations, applications of remote sensing, and field programs. Students will undertake evaluation of recent literature or an independent research paper.

title: Remote Sensing of the Environment
description: A quantitative overview of atmospheric remote sensing concentrating on tropospheric phenomena.  Emphasis is placed on developing a basic theoretical foundation as well as detailed examination of selected contemporary problems in this rapidly evolving field.

title: Scientific Writing and Oral Presentation
description: Writing and oral skills for scientific communication. The course emphases identifying and communicating the story intended for proposals, theses and dissertations, papers, and conference presentations.

title: Graduate Special Topics
description: Intensive work in a specific area of meteorology.

title: Graduate Seminar
description: Presentation of scholarly works of faculty, graduate students, and external scientists.  Repeatable for up to 10 credit hours.

title: Continuing Registration: Ph.D.
description: Continuing registration needed for PhD students.

title: Introduction to Prevention & Health Promotion in Sports Medicine
description: This course focuses on important aspects for prevention and health promotion of active populations. It is also intended as an introduction course to athletic training. If you are interested in the athletic training program, please go to the AT website to review program requirements and visit the College of Health advising web page to schedule an appointment with an advisor. The lab ATSM 3310 is required for all AT applicants. If you choose to take the lab, you will practice clinical skills and implement the knowledge you learned during the didactic course.

title: Global Engagement in Sports Medicine
description: This course explores healthcare delivery and athletic training in a global context through a supervised learning abroad experience. Students engage in classroom instruction and clinical observation to examine healthcare systems, health status, and cultural practices in an international setting. This course emphasizes the development of cultural competence, reflective clinical practice, and professional growth. Faculty-led discussions will compare healthcare models and athletic training roles across cultures. Students will participate in cultural immersion activities and collaborate with international peers and professionals to enhance understanding of global sports medicine.

title: Introduction to Clinical Exam and Rehab
description: This course will provide students foundational knowledge to injury prevention, clinical examination and diagnosis, and therapeutic strategies. Students will learn about and use various tests and measures to evaluate a patient’s status. Students will learn how to develop a plan of care using techniques and interventions commonly practiced in athletic training.

title: Therapeutic Modalities in Athletic Training
description: This course will develop comprehension and understanding of the physiological and clinical aspects of therapeutic modalities, specifically, theory and application of heat, cold, light and laser, ultrasound, electromagnetic and mechanical energy, and electrical stimulation in treatment and rehabilitation procedures will be performed.

title: Evaluation and Rehabilitation of Lower Extremity Injuries
description: This course will introduce students to the prevention, examination, and rehabilitation of lower extremity injuries and other sports-related conditions. In particular, using knowledge of anatomy and physiology, this course will cover the foot, ankle, knee, and thigh regions.

title: Evaluation and Rehabilitation of Upper Extremity and Head Injuries
description: This course will introduce students to the prevention, evaluation, and rehabilitation of upper extremity injuries and other sports-related conditions. In particular, using knowledge of anatomy and physiology, this course will cover the head, shoulder, elbow, wrist and hand regions.

title: Evaluation and Rehabilitation of Spine, Pelvis, and Hip Injuries
description: This course will introduce students to the prevention, examination and rehabilitation of head, spine, hip and pelvic injuries and other sports-related conditions. In particular, using knowledge of anatomy and physiology, this course will cover the cervical, thoracic and lumbar spine regions, the hip and pelvis.

title: Gross Anatomy
description: Gross anatomy of the anterior and posterior abdominal walls, the superficial and deep back muscles, lower extremities, head, neck, thorax, and upper extremities.  This course includes 2 credit hours for dissection laboratory.

title: Advanced Rehabilitation and Manual Therapy
description: This course will introduce physiology concepts and practical skills training of various types of advanced rehabilitation and manual therapy interventions commonly used in athletic training practice.

title: Diagnostics in Athletic Training
description: This course will introduce principles and interpretation of diagnostic imaging and laboratory testing in athletic training. This course will emphasize radiography, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), musculoskeletal (MSK) ultrasound, and computed tomography (CT) imaging techniques and laboratory blood tests.

title: Introduction to Acute Care
description: This course focuses on emergency care in the context of athletic training clinical practice. This course is intended to introduce students to the process of recognition and management of emergent medical situations occurring during athletic practices or competitions. This course is designed on the assumption that the athletic trainer will be the initial responder to emergent medical situations on the field of play / athletic venue. The athletic trainer will need to manage and stabilize patients to the best of their ability until the arrival of emergency medical personnel or more highly trained providers. Content for this course will include but not be limited to preparation of emergency action plans, bloodborne pathogen/exposure control plans, physical examination of the critically injured athlete, acute management of fractures, acute control of bleeding, on the field examination of a potential concussion, certification or re-certification in BLS for Health Care Providers.

title: Advanced Acute Care
description: This course focuses on emergency care in the context of athletic training clinical practice. This course is intended to further students’ knowledge and skills related to the process of recognition and management of emergent medical situations occurring during athletic practices or competitions. This course is designed on the assumption that the athletic trainer will be the initial responder to emergent medical situations on the field of play / athletic venue. The athletic trainer will need to manage and stabilize patients to the best of their ability until the arrival of emergency medical personnel or more highly trained providers. Content for this course will include but not be limited to preparation of emergency action plans, blood borne pathogen / exposure control plans, physical examination of the critically injured athlete, acute management of fractures, acute control of bleeding, on the field examination of potential concussion, certification or re-certification in BLS for Health Care Providers.

title: Taping and Bracing
description: This course provides students with the knowledge and hands-on skills to select, apply, evaluate, and modify standard and advanced protective equipment, taping, wrapping, bracing, padding, and custom devices to support safe participation in sport and physical activity. Emphasis is placed on injury prevention and risk minimization as students explore the purposes and techniques of various taping and bracing methods used to protect and support injured areas. Advanced applications are covered across diverse athletic training settings to prepare students for making clinical decisions and implementing practical solutions in real-world scenarios.

title: Advanced Taping and Bracing
description: This class is designed to enhance the students' understanding of purposes and methods of using various taping and bracing techniques to support and protect injuries to bones, ligaments, tendons, muscles, nerves, and joints of the body. Advanced taping and bracing techniques are presented for all of the various athletic training settings.

title: Aspects of Injury and Illness Prevention
description: This course focuses on important aspects of injury prevention and screenings of active populations. Prevention concepts from a pre-participation exam, functional movement screenings, and injury prevention programs will be discussed and performed in practical laboratory sessions.

title: Clinical Seminar in Sports Medicine I
description: This course will be a seminar of presentations on current topics, theories and techniques commonly used in the field of sports medicine, physical therapy, rehabilitative science and clinical orthopedics. Laboratory experiences will follow didactic presentations when appropriate.

title: Clinical Seminar in Sports Medicine II
description: This course will be a seminar of presentations on current topics, theories and techniques commonly used in the field of sports medicine, physical therapy, rehabilitative science and clinical orthopedics. Laboratory experiences will follow didactic presentations when appropriate.

title: Behavioral Health Considerations
description: This course will focus on referral, recognition, and management of psychological conditions or states that will affect clients/athletes and the athletic training in the athletic training setting.

title: General Medical Conditions in Athletic Training
description: This course will examine general medical conditions commonly evaluated and treated in athletic training. Students will learn to recognize signs and symptoms, evaluation techniques, common treatment patterns for general medical conditions. Students will also learn when to refer to a more advanced medical provider.

title: Pharmacology in Athletic Training
description: This course will focus on the basic principles of pharmacology including pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of therapeutic medications or supplements commonly encountered in athletic training and sports performance settings. Students learn how to implement these principles into a pharmaceutical management plan.

title: Current Trends in Sports Medicine
description: Students will prepare presentations on contemporary issues in athletic training and sports medicine.  Students will research and critique the literature in an area of sports medicine.  The purpose of the class is to create an intellectual dialogue about a contemporary issue in sports medicine.

title: Data Acquisition in Sports Medicine
description: This course is concerned with current theory and application of various data acquisition techniques use in sports medicine research and clinical biomechanics.  Research techniques and instrumentation used in Physical Therapy, Orthopedics, Clinical Rehabilitative Science, and Exercise Physiology will be included.

title: Pathology and Rehabilitation of Athletic Injuries
description: This course presents an overview of the etiology for head and neck, upper and lower extremity, and trunk injuries.  Also included are the contemporary therapeutic exercise protocols for each of these injuries.

title: Organization and Administration in Sports Medicine
description: This course will present organizational and administrative items commonly encountered amongst clinical healthcare professionals in a variety of settings. The objective is to help the student to be prepared for the organizational tasks that are required of an Athletic Trainer in various settings. In addition, we will work on creating ethical and efficient practice, policies and procedures. Students will also be exposed to human resource services and hiring practices.

title: Introduction to Evidence Based Practice
description: This course will educate students on the foundation skills of evidence-based practice as it pertains to athletic training. Students will learn and apply these foundations skills of evidence-based practice into the clinical decision-making process incorporating the best evidence, patient values, and clinical experiences.

title: Advanced Evidence-Based Practice
description: This course will apply evidence-based practice principles in clinical practice settings. Critical analysis of the levels of evidence supporting various special tests and clinical evaluation skills will be a major component of this course. Throughout this course, students will explore a variety of methods for gathering and generating evidence relevant to clinical practice.

title: Foundations of Interprofessional Education
description: This course introduces the foundations of interprofessional collaborative practice. Students will engage in self-directed learning with other disciplines through the exploration of values and ethics for interprofessional practice, roles and responsibilities of health care providers, interprofessional communication strategies, and the development of high functioning teams in health care. This course is designed to provide a framework for deliberately working together with other health professionals to maintain a climate of mutual respect and shared values in today’s complex health care environment, with the common goal of improving patient outcomes.

title: Simulations in Athletic Training
description: Students will practice and be assessed on athletic training patient management skills using simulations. These simulations will be designed to help students progress from a novice student to an advanced beginner ready for future clinical education experiences. Students will learn how to critically think and solve problems in complex situations.

title: Clinical Experience I
description: Students engage in a part-time clinical placement, with duration varying based on site-specific requirements. Under the direct supervision of clinical preceptors, students begin applying patient management skills in real-world clinical settings. Through observation and progressively supervised patient encounters, students develop foundational competencies in clinical practice. Emphasis will also be placed on integrating and advancing professional behaviors essential to effective healthcare delivery.This course is one of three required part-time clinical education courses in the Master of Athletic Training curriculum. The structure of each course is similar. However, students should progress in the knowledge they apply from their didactic courses and in their clinical skills. Students should set goals with each preceptor to ensure their clinical progress and success.

title: Clinical Experience II
description: Students engage in a part-time clinical placement, with duration varying based on site-specific requirements. Under the direct supervision of clinical preceptors, students begin applying patient management skills in real-world clinical settings. Through observation and progressively supervised patient encounters, students will develop foundational competencies in clinical practice. Emphasis will also be placed on integrating and advancing professional behaviors essential to effective healthcare delivery.This is course two of three required part-time clinical education courses in the Master of Athletic Training curriculum. The structure of each course is similar. However, students should progress in the knowledge they apply from their didactic courses and in their clinical skills. Students should set goals with each preceptor to ensure their clinical progress and success.

title: Clinical Experience III
description: Students will engage in a part-time clinical placement, with duration varying based on site-specific requirements. Under the direct supervision of clinical preceptors, students begin applying patient management skills in real-world clinical settings. Through observation and progressively supervised patient encounters, students develop foundational competencies in clinical practice. Emphasis also on integrating and advancing professional behaviors essential to effective healthcare delivery.This is course three of three required part-time clinical education courses in the Master of Athletic Training curriculum. The structure of each course is similar. However, students should progress in the knowledge they apply from their didactic courses and in their clinical skills. Students should set goals with each preceptor to ensure their clinical progress and success.

title: Clinical Experience IV
description: In this course, students can be assigned optional part-time clinical placements, with duration varying based on site-specific requirements. Under the direct supervision of clinical preceptors, students begin applying patient management skills in real-world clinical settings. Through observation and progressively supervised patient encounters, students develop foundational competencies in clinical practice. Emphasis placed on integrating and advancing professional behaviors essential to effective healthcare delivery.This course can only be taken after the three required clinical education courses have been satisfied (ATSM 6520, 6522, 6523).

title: Clinical Internship I
description: One full-time clinical placements, 15 weeks in length. Students practice patient management skills in the clinical environment under the direct supervision of clinical preceptors. Students observe and begin to perform supervised patient encounters. The student will also begin to integrate and progressively develop a variety of skills critical for developing appropriate professionalism.

title: Clinical Internship II
description: Two full-time clinical placements, 7-8 weeks in length. Students practice patient management skills in the clinical environment under the direct supervision of clinical preceptors. Students observe and begin to perform supervised patient encounters. The student will also begin to integrate and progressively develop a variety of skills critical for developing appropriate professionalism.

title: Athletic Training and Healthcare Delivery
description: This course introduces students to the athletic training profession by exploring its history, roles, responsibilities, and current state within the broader sports medicine team. Emphasis is placed on the athletic trainer’s role in healthcare delivery, including professional development, documentation, insurance, reimbursement, and public health. Students gain foundational knowledge of healthcare systems, preparing them to understand and navigate the complexities of athletic training in modern clinical practice.

title: Best Practice in Health Care Delivery
description: This course focuses on healthcare delivery system and an athletic trainers’ role. Students will discover key concepts in health care delivery mechanisms, insurance, reimbursement, documentation, public health, quality assurance and quality improvement.

title: Communication Strategies in Health Care
description: It is important that athletic trainers communicate effectively and appropriately with patients, family members, coaches, administrators, other healthcare providers, and policies makers. This course will focus on proper verbal and non-verbal communication strategies in athletic training and health care.

title: The Adaptive Athlete in Sports Medicine
description: This course will explore common diagnoses and impairments of athletes who compete in adaptive sports through organizations including, but not limited, to the Paralympics, the Special Olympics, the Deaf Olympics, and other worldwide organizations. Students will learn about working with adaptive athletes and the similarities and differences between special populations and their able-bodied counterparts.

title: Health Care Informatics
description: This course will focus on health care informatics to aid in the administration and delivery of patient care and to explore quality assurance and quality improvement to enhance patient care. Students will learn about the details associated with electronic medical records and their ability to be a source of data for improving patient care. Other data from biometric and physiological monitoring systems will be explored to be used in effective preventative measures, clinical interventions, and performance enhancement.

title: Athletic Training Patient and Professional Advocacy
description: This course will focus on how athletic trainers can advocate for their patients and populations they care for and communities they work in. Students will also discuss the current professional mission and goals of athletic training and how they can advocate for these causes.

title: Transition to Practice
description: This course will focus on key skills to continue a life-time of development as students transition from novice clinicians to expert athletic trainers. In particular students in this course will discuss the qualities of a good mentor, self-reflection, professional development, and administration duties of an athletic trainer.

title: Ethics in Athletic Training
description: This course focuses on ethical practices in athletic training. Ethical practices in health care delivery will be explored through case-based learning and discussions. Key foundational concepts in ethics and boundaries within health care delivery will also be explored.

title: Sports Medicine Symposium
description: The Sports Medicine Symposium is designed to introduce students to leaders in the field of Sports Medicine. Both local and national leaders in the field will be invited to campus to provide a description to their interest.

title: Clinical Capstone in Athletic Training
description: This clinical capstone project focuses on transitioning to a professional athletic trainer and making important decisions that impact patients' health care. Students will complete a capstone project that emphasizes evidence practice, quality improvement, and the inter-professional health care team.

title: Master's Internship
description: Students will work with professionals in the field to gain experience as practicing exercise and sport professionals.

title: Global Engagement in Sports Medicine
description: This course, offered through the Learning Abroad Office, will offer students the opportunity to experience health care in a diverse culture as a required participant of the learning abroad program associated with this course. Students will learn about healthcare delivery in this diverse culture. Students will practice culture competence as a healthcare provider. This will allow students to incorporate these practices in their future career. Practicum study abroad to learn about the health care delivery system, health status of the people, and athletic training in a different culture through classroom education and observed clinical experiences. Students meet with faculty in evenings to discuss observations and compare health care delivery systems between countries, as well as athletic training roles and health status of each population. Opportunities provided to socialize with students and faculty in destination and for sightseeing.

title: Journal Readings
description: To provide students with opportunities to critically evaluate the research literature for the purpose of formulating and developing their own research questions.

title: Ballet I for Non-Majors
description: A foundation course covering the basic vocabulary, technique, and styles of ballet.

title: Ballet II for Non-Majors
description: Ballet Technique for non-majors with some experience in ballet.

title: Elements of Music
description: Study of music as it relates to classical ballet.

title: Special Topics
description: Variable subject matter.  Concentrated studies and examination of dance styles and/or to broaden experience and understanding of historical roots, culture, movement and aesthetics of selected dance genres, including modern dance, world dances, folk dances, ethnic dances and specialized dance techniques and methodologies.

title: Pointe/Variations I
description: Study of pointe work with an emphasis on building strength, coordination, and stability.

title: Ballet Technique I
description: Ballet technique for first-year majors.

title: Character Dance I
description: First semester of a two-semester sequence for departmental majors. Must be taken consecutively in the same academic year. Folk dance study based on ethnic (character) dances within a ballet performance context.

title: Character Dance II
description: Second semester of a two-semester sequence that must be taken in the same academic year. Theatrical dance based on ethnic and folk dances within a ballet performance context.

title: Contemporary Dance Techniques I
description: This course is designed for ballet majors to provide technical training in contemporary dance techniques. Principles of alignment, connectivity, and expression are incorporated. Techniques taught may include but are not limited to: varied modern and post-modern dance forms, somatic practices, forms from the Afro-Caribbean diaspora, and varied improvisational forms.

title: Contemporary Dance Techniques II
description: This course, the second, of a two-semester sequence following BALLE 2320 for ballet majors, continues to focus on technical training in contemporary dance techniques. Principles of alignment, connectivity, and expression are incorporated. Techniques taught may include but are not limited to: varied modern and post-modern dance forms, somatic practices, forms from the Afro-Caribbean diaspora, and varied improvisational forms.

title: Jazz I: Histories and Contemporary Forms
description: In this course, students will develop techniques of jazz dance. Emphasis is placed upon cultural and historical understanding, styling, rhythm, musicality, and phrasing. It draws from the origins of American jazz dance including vernacular jazz forms, influences from the Afro-Caribbean Diaspora, and Broadway, as well as urban, funk, and contemporary influences.

title: Ballet Class Music: A Tutorial for Ballet Teachers
description: This course will include a comprehensive analysis of the predominant music/dance rhythms and forms commonly used in the ballet technique class. The students will use about 45% of their class time in studio labs developing skills in musically using and communicating established music/dance rhythms and forms. It is expected that they will com to understand the relationship that applicable musical elements have with ballet technique and artistry. This should help them understand and apply these skills in their own ballet technique experience as well as in their role as a ballet pedagogue.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study projects for lower-division students.

title: Dance Improvisation to Choreography
description: This course is designed to provide students an opportunity to increase their improvisational dance movement ability. In this class students will have opportunities to explore free movement, expand their reflection instincts, and to freely express themselves.

title: Pilates Mat Class
description: Pilates exercises will be introduced, including all the Pilates Principles (control, proper breath, centering, coordination and concentration and oppositional energy) for the improvement of body awareness and aesthetics.

title: Pointe/Variations II
description: Pointe work with an emphasis on building strength, control, and movement quality. Variations from the classical repertory.

title: Ballet Technique II
description: Upper-division ballet technique for Ballet Majors.

title: Pilates Reformer and Apparatus Class
description: Pilates principles (control, proper breath, centering, coordination and concentration and oppositional energy) will be focused on and put to use on the reformer, cadillac and low chair to enhance dancers' body awareness and aesthetics.

title: Special Topics
description: Variable subject matter.  Concentrated studies and examination of dance styles and/or methods to broaden experience and understanding of historical roots, culture, movement and aesthetics of selected dance genres, including world dances, folk dances, ethnic dances and specialized dance techniques and methodologies.

title: Special Topics: Dance Techniques
description: This is an open forum techniques course that offers students alternate movement practices from ones taught more consistently in the School of Dance. The course focuses on gaining new movement pathways and dance skills, and/or mastery of technical facility, qualitative expression, and bodily connectivity in a variety of dance forms. Students build technical and performance skills through exposure to increasingly complex movement material. This course may introduce alternate movement practice that help expand each dancers art horizon. Techniques taught can include but are not limited to: varied alternate ballet and contemporary forms, contact improvisation, forms from the Afro-Caribbean diaspora, varied somatic practices, theater practices, forms from the Pacific Islands diaspora, among others.

title: Pointe/Variations III
description: Advanced pointe technique with the study of variations from the classical repertory.

title: Pointe Methodology
description: This course introduces the methodology of teaching pointe technique for classical ballet. Students learn how to analyze the specifics of basic ballet movements performed on pointe, pedagogically sound class progressions related to strengthening, and how to design and develop learning outcomes. The class is taught in a lecture/lab format including micro-teaching experiences.

title: Ballet Technique III
description: Advanced ballet technique study for ballet majors.

title: Anatomy for Dance
description: This course provides a study of musculoskeletal anatomy and kinesiological analysis applied specifically to the art of dance. The course prepares the student to understand common musculoskeletal idiosyncrasies, imbalances, and injuries, and begin to address them in relation to dance training, conditioning, and somatic-based approaches. Meets with DANC 4510 and DANC 6610.

title: Experiential Anatomy for Dance
description: This course builds upon the' detailed musculoskeletal knowledge covered in the Anatomy for Dance (BALLE 4401) course. The focus is for students to embody the material presented in BALLE 4401, further developing, analyzing, and fine-tuning individualized conditioning programs through regular practice, observation, and reflection. Students establish a regular embodied practice. For the 2 credit option, students will additionally gain skills in teaching/coaching others. Meets with DANC 4511 and DANC 6620.

title: Repertoire
description: This is a studio-based course, designed to train dancers to work in a cohesive and responsible way as a group. Skills of learning and adapting choreography are developed. Classical, neoclassical, and/or contemporary choreography (varies each term) will be incorporated, thus familiarizing students with professional repertoire.

title: Repertoire: Professional
description: Repertoire credit for professional training as a member or apprentice with a professional company. Professional training credits must be approved by the School of Dance and overseen by a member of the faculty.

title: Pointe/Variations IV
description: Advanced pointe and variations for Utah Ballet Company.

title: Traditional Male Ballet Technique and Variations
description: This course focuses on the study of traditional male ballet technique and variations, including allegro jumps and turns.

title: Classical Ballet Partnering
description: This course focuses on traditional classical ballet partnering techniques.

title: Contemporary Partnering
description: This course focuses on contemporary partnering techniques.

title: Partnering Repertoire
description: This course focuses on partnering sequences and/or excerpts from established classical ballet (including grand pas de deux) and/or contemporary ballet repertoire.

title: Partnering: Professional
description: Ballet majors engaged in professional training as a member or apprentice with a professional company may register for this course to receive credit for the experience they gain in partnering skills during the professional training period. Professional training credits must be approved by the School of Dance prior to registration and supervised by a member of the faculty.

title: Ballet Technique IV
description: Advanced ballet technique for Utah Ballet Company.

title: Performance Credit: Professional
description: Performance credit for performances with a professional company. Professional performance credits must be approved by the School of Dance and overseen by a member of the faculty.

title: Choreography I
description: Development of choreographic skills through the study of theory, music, and the great choreographic masterpieces. Individual assignments are given with group critique and discussion during lab sessions.

title: Choreography II
description: Continuation of BALLE 4700 to be taken in the same academic year. Development of choreographic skills through the study of theory, music, and the great choreographic masterpieces. Individual assignments are given with group critique and discussion during lab sessions.

title: Pointe/Variations: Professional
description: Advanced pointe and variations credit for professional training as a member or apprentice with a professional company. Professional training credits must be approved by the School of Dance and overseen by a member of the faculty.

title: Traditional Male Ballet Technique and Variations: Professional
description: Ballet majors engaged in professional training as a member or apprentice with a professional company may register for this course to receive credit for the experience they gain in traditional male ballet technique and variations during the professional training period. Professional training credits must be approved by the School of Dance prior to registration and supervised by a member of the faculty.

title: Choreography III and Production
description: Practical application of the process of choreography as it moves from studio to stage. In addition to demonstrating acquired choreographic knowledge, students gain experience in staging, group management, and the implementation of technical aspects of theatrical performance such as lighting design and costuming. Final projects will result in a formal performance.

title: Ballet Pedagogy I
description: This course covers techniques for teaching creative movement and for introducing classical ballet to children age 3-9. Students learn how to analyze technique progression, utilize the vocabulary of age-appropriate ballet movements, and develop teaching goals and objectives. The course is taught in a lecture and lab format.

title: Ballet Pedagogy II
description: This course is a continuation of BALLE 4780 and offers a more in-depth examination of the art of teaching classical ballet. It specifically includes material for ages 10-17. The student will: acquire techniques for teaching age-appropriate ballet movement vocabulary; learn how to incorporate class progression in the studio; develop a teaching curriculum, accompanying syllabi, and lesson plans for sequential levels of ballet technique.

title: Ballet Technique: Professional
description: Advanced ballet technique credit for professional training as a member or apprentice with a professional company. Professional training credits must be approved by the School of Dance and overseen by a member of the faculty.

title: Independent Study General
description: For work on projects by ballet majors not included in other independent study courses.

title: Seminar in Dance Studies
description: Utilizing a Dance Studies approach and focusing on current dance scholarship, the course emphasizes the study of movement as a window into larger cultural processes and historical frameworks. This course takes a broad interdisciplinary approach to dance scholarship, and serves as an ongoing forum for discussion on current topics and targeted areas of dance practice, research and scholarship.

title: Dance Writing
description: This course focuses on dance criticism, both historically and critically, and ways in which dance is represented by and connected to writing in a variety of settings and circumstances. Students are provided with practical writing tools for application in careers in the field of dance and the arts, and they are offered frameworks for thinking about how writing shapes and influences our engagement with dance.

title: Teaching Practicum: Ballet (Honors)
description: Provides a teaching opportunity or apprenticeship with a written component and assessment.  Honors students in the teaching emphasis register for a minimum of 2 credit hours and actively teach or observe classes from two hours weekly.  Honors students will be required to defend opinions and write on several topics pertinent to teaching ballet for levels determined by the advisor.  The quality of the writing, analysis, comparisons, and criticisms must be at a high level.

title: Teaching Practicum: Other (Honors)
description: This course provides a teaching opportunity or apprenticeship with a written component and assessment. Honors students pursuing the teaching emphasis register for a minimum of 2 credit hours and actively teach and/or observe classes for two hours weekly. Honors students are required to defend opinions and write on several topics pertinent to teaching dance. The quality of the writing, analysis, comparisons, and criticisms must be at a high level.

title: Teaching Practicum Ballet
description: This course offers an advanced teaching experience that builds upon the student's previous pedagogical coursework further developing her/his teaching expertise in ballet technique. It can be done either as an on-campus or off-campus experience under the supervision of the teaching emphasis faculty.

title: Teaching Practicum: Other
description: This course offers an advanced teaching experience that builds upon the student's previous pedagogical coursework further developing her/his teaching expertise in teaching a dance genre other than ballet technique. It can be done either as an on-campus or off-campus experience under the supervision of the teaching emphasis faculty.

title: Senior Capstone
description: The purpose of this course is to give students an opportunity to apply all that they have learned in previous courses in the BFA Ballet program. Ideally, they will apply the skills and concepts they have learned to a real-world project related to ballet. In this manner, students will derive greater benefit from the experience, as they will better appreciate the importance of the knowledge they have acquired.

title: Senior Capstone Honors
description: This course is for senior students wishing to do their senior capstone in choreography. Students must have created at least two past works that had good response from faculty. Students will be required to have their proposal approved by the faculty and will fully produce their work, engaging in choreographing, costuming, and lighting this work for a Showcase production. Students will be required to keep a journal of the creative process and turn this into a faculty mentor in order to receive a grade.

title: Honors Thesis/Project
description: Restricted to students in the Honors Program working on their Honors degree.

title: Pointe/Variations: Graduate
description: Advanced pointe/variations study for graduate students.

title: Ballet Technique: Graduate
description: Advanced ballet technique study for graduate students.

title: Repertoire: Graduate
description: This studio-based course trains dancers how to work in a cohesive and responsible way as a group. Students develop skills learning and adapting choreography. Classical, neo-classical, or original choreography (varies each term) may be incorporated for this purpose, thus also serving an additional purpose of familiarizing dancers with repertoire. Graduate students are expected to conduct historical research as relevant to the work and assume leadership roles during the rehearsal and staging processes.

title: Men's Class: Graduate
description: Advanced men's class study for graduate students.

title: Partner Adagio: Graduate
description: Advanced partnering skills for ballet. Includes complex turns and lifts. Coaching of partnering in contemporary works as well as grand pas de deux from the classical repertoire.

title: Ballet Pedagogy I
description: This course provides graduate students with a theoretical base, practical application of principles, and mentoring in the teaching of ballet.

title: Ballet Pedagogy II
description: This course builds upon topics covered in BALLE 6780, and focuses on providing graduate students with a theoretical base, practical application of principles, and mentoring in the teaching of dance.

title: Scholarly Writing
description: This graduate level course serves as an overview of a variety of approaches to creating and maintaining a scholarly writing practice.

title: Graduate Project
description: In this graduate level independent study course, students design a syllabus and work closely with a faculty mentor to complete a project. Projects may cover a range of student interests including, but not limited to, choreography, performance, pedagogy, production, scholarly inquiry, etc.

title: Typography 1: Form And Space
description: This studio course introduces students to the basic elements and principles of typography, form and space. Students will gain an understanding of typography as an integral part of design and develop basic typesetting skills.

title: Papermaking: Fiber, Process & Creative Application
description: In this course, students produce a variety of handmade papers from plant fibers like cotton, abaca, flax, and hemp. Students learn and practice the entire papermaking process: cooking plant material, pulping fibers in a Hollander Beater, pulling sheets using a traditional mould and deckle, and pressing and drying finished sheets. The class also makes Kozo paper in the Japanese style, which differs significantly from Western papermaking. The course includes introductions to lamination, pigmentation, pulp painting, sculptural methods, and other handmade paper processes, as well as a visit to the Rare Books collection to observe handmade paper in exemplars ranging from incunabula to contemporary artists' books. Throughout the semester, students learn to refine craft and assess the qualities of handmade paper as they work collaboratively to produce editioned paper, some of which is used in independent art projects incorporating additional media.

title: Book Arts: Letterpress Printing I
description: Using movable type, linoleum blocks, pressure print matrices, collagraphs, photoengraving, and photopolymer plates, students design and print several editions on hand-operated letterpresses. Through discussion and critique, students learn basic elements of design and typography with the option of integrating other printing processes into letterpress work. Typically, students participate in a class print exchange. The class also views letterpress work from the Marriott Library’s Rare Books Collection.

title: Book Arts: Bookbinding I
description: This course explores the basic elements of bookbinding, including structure, materials, tools, and techniques. Students construct a variety of book forms and enclosures, and complete creative final projects that integrate skills learned during the semester with original concept and design. Modern and historical structures from Eastern and Western traditions are considered within the context of the history of the book. In this introductory class, discussion, critique, readings, and lectures support hands-on learning.

title: Book Design & Production
description: This studio course explores the principles and practices of book planning, design, and production. The meaning of visible language is investigated through typography, layout, and design. Students publish projects through varied means of production, using both digital and analog methods. The course covers the entirety of book production, including concept development, pre-press, and sending projects to print. The class also utilizes Marriott Library’s Rare Books Collection to view and discuss examples of book design throughout history.

title: Artists' Books I
description: This course covers content development, bookmaking, basic book design, integration of various media, low-tech image-making processes, text generation, and the functionality of various bookmaking materials. Students create a library of book models through hands-on bookmaking demonstrations with provided kits and cultivate individual book projects around these models, based on each assignment's conceptual, material, and structural needs. Discussions around artists’ books from the Marriott Library’s collections and assigned readings as well as in-class exercises directly inform student work. The final project is student-directed and instructor-supported as individual concepts are developed and the appropriate production method, structure, and materials are identified, customized, applied, and produced.

title: The Printed Book: Intermediate Binding & Letterpress
description: Students who have completed Bookbinding I and/or Letterpress I will build on foundational skills through completion of a student-directed, semester-length project. Intermediate concepts and skills in binding and letterpress as well as other modes of printing are closely examined through demonstrations, lectures, presentations, discussions, and critiques. The class also provides the opportunity to hone design, writing, and image-making skills and techniques through shorter projects and in-class exercises.

title: Book Arts: Letterpress Printing III
description: This class provides the opportunity to become immersed in letterpress printing. Students work with the instructor to design their syllabus.  Using the Rare Books collections as a resource, students research individual interests and produce work inspired by master binders' and book artists' standards, formats, techniques, approach, and concepts.  Instructor provides instruction in particular methods according to students' needs.  Students are held to very rigorous standards of craftsmanship and project development.

title: Book Arts: Bookbinding III
description: This class provides the opportunity to become immersed in bookbinding.  Students work with the instructor to design their program of study.  Using the Rare Books collections as a resource, students research individual interests and produce work inspired by master binders' and book artists' standards, formats techniques, approach, and concepts.  Instructor provides instruction in particular methods according to students' needs.  Students are held to very rigorous standards of craftsmanship and project development.

title: Artists' Books II
description: Building on skills acquired in Artists' Books I, this course provides the opportunity for higher-level research, ideation, conceptualization, structural innovation, material exploration, and expansion of production modalities. In addition to completing several small projects early in the semester, the intermediate student, in dialogue with the instructor(s), designs a semester-length project cultivated around exemplars and models. Through class critique and discussion, appropriate formats for specified concepts are identified, which the student can then adapt and apply in the production of a self-authored artist's book. Critical theory, content development, book design, integration of various media, low-tech image making and printing processes, and the functionality of various bookmaking materials are addressed throughout the course. A student with previous letterpress experience may opt to produce a letterpress edition and will be advised by instructor(s) regarding printing concerns.

title: Career Exploration
description: The goal of this course is to provide a Career Development Plan that takes students through our 4-step Career Planning Model: Self-Discovery, Exploration, Decision Making, Implementation. This course will help students explore and prepare for career paths in business through assessments and activities. Upon completion, students will have discovered their talents, interests and work values and bring this into their plan. They will explore potential career paths and make important career decisions with assistance from a Business Career Services’ Coach. They will then develop an action-plan to work towards their career goals while in school and implement their job search by developing the necessary tools needed to be a competitive candidate.

title: Frameworks for Business Problems
description: This course is the first of a two-course sequence designed to present you with real problems of the type you are likely to encounter in the business world and to provide you with the tools and concepts you can use to address those problems. The sequence covers foundational and integrated concepts from critical thinking, data management, and statistical modeling and data analytics. This first course includes modules on critical thinking, information systems, data collection, data management, data visualization and spreadsheet modeling. Case studies will be incorporated throughout the class to emphasize application of course to real business situations and to integrate topics.

title: Frameworks for Business Problems II
description: This course is a continuation of Business Problems I and is designed to present you with real problems of the type you are likely to encounter in the business world and to provide you with the tools and concepts you can use to address those problems. Course topics include a review of critical thinking and data collection methods, probability and probability distributions, sampling, statistical inference, regression (including logistics regression) and predictive analytics and basic machine learning. Case studies will again be incorporated throughout the class to emphasize application of course to real business situations and to integrate topics.

title: Value, Impact, Business and Entreprenuership (VIBE)
description: This course focuses on how individuals innovate to create and capture value, with a focus on the power of individuals to pursue new ideas through forming and joining profit seeking and/or mission driven organizations. In particular, we will study how individuals decide to carry out specific work within organizations and/or decide to start up new organizations to create value. Economics, strategy, entrepreneurship, psychology, sociology and other social and behavioral sciences help us understand how and why some value creation efforts succeed and others fail. This course focuses not only on abstract concepts, but on your own unique ability to create value through the academic and experiential path you choose. You will determine an area of interest (domain) and apply the course concepts each week as you explore potentially high-value ways to integrate your studies, your work, and your passions.

title: Business Fundamentals - Accounting
description: Part of Business Fundamentals, an integrated survey of foundational business concepts. The Accounting component provides an introductory view of accounting, focusing on those who use accounting information to interpret financial data. It introduces students to the role of accounting and the various individuals who rely on accounting within a business. Students learn the fundamentals of accounting, with a focus on understanding and using information provided within financial statements and how these statements are used by various stakeholders, including investors and executives of the company.

title: Business Fundamentals - Management
description: Part of Business Fundamentals, an integrated survey of foundational business concepts. The Management component will introduce students to the field of management. Businesses depend upon people with the ability to organize how work gets done. In modern organizations, this task falls to managers. Managers come in many forms, but they have one core responsibility in common: they need to get the right people to the right place at the right time to do the right job. Every day, managers face questions such as: How do you find and attract the right people with the right skills? How do you motivate them? How do you design work? How do you think critically and make effective decisions in organizations? What ethical obligations do you have to stakeholders, and how can you meet them while respecting your own values? Successful businesses rely on their managers’ abilities to approach these questions with wisdom and skill.

title: Business Fundamentals - Marketing
description: Part of Business Fundamentals, an integrated survey of foundational business concepts. The Marketing component introduces strategies and tactics designed to generate revenue and profit for a firm. Its purpose is to help students understand markets and methods for introducing and managing products and services, setting prices, developing promotions, and structuring distribution.

title: Pathways to Community Engagement
description: The Pathways to Community Engagement course will serve as a foundational introduction to the myriad opportunities individuals have to participate as active citizens. Students in this course will explore and examine the history and key tenets of community engagement, develop an understanding of civic competencies, and participate in community-engaged learning experiences.

title: Introduction to Civic Leadership
description: Many communities today face systemic challenges related to economic instability, environmental change, and access issues to healthcare, education, and other opportunities. This course introduces theoretical and practical approaches to community-based work. Students explore frameworks of leadership, asset-based community development, and collective action, and engage in a project that addresses a community-identified issue. Through structured reflection and engagement, students develop an understanding of their individual civic values, group dynamics, and ethical community partnerships. Emphasis is placed on systems thinking, leadership development, and effective collaboration within varied community contexts.

title: Bennion Center Alternative Break
description: This community engaged learning course is the academic component to the Bennion Center’s Alternative Break (AB) experiences. Each section of this course and its corresponding AB trip is collaboratively designed by students, faculty, and staff, at the University of Utah to address social, economic, political, and environmental issues through education, community engagement, and travel. Alternative Break teams and this course will address critical community issues such as food access, illiteracy, environmental degradation, gender, society equity/justice, animal welfare, discrimination, inadequate housing, hunger, and poverty in cities and communities different from their own. The course provides transformative deeply engaged community learning experiences that help students develop their civic competencies and inspire them to generate new ideas, global perspectives, and empathy, that fosters active and engaged citizenship. Students in this course will learn and develop: 1) the four civic competencies outlined by the Bennion Center; 2) the components of quality Alternative Break experiences—Strong Direct Service, Education, Orientation, Training, Reflection, Reorientation, Diversity, and Alcohol/Drug-free; 3) how to deeply reflect on learning experiences individually and as a team; 4) the root causes and potential solutions to social, economic, environmental, and political problems facing communities; and 5) the important empathy and perspective gleaned from travel to and working with diverse communities that are different than their own.

title: Costa Rica: Community, Systems, and Development
description: This is a community engaged learning abroad course (CELA) to explore the power of community engagement in an international context. We will utilize the history, culture, and the civic issues affecting Costa Rica, including sustainability, as a living learning journey. The course is composed of weekly on-campus classes and a one week intensive in-country experience in Costa Rica over spring break in March. This is a collaboration between The Lowell Bennion Community Service Center, Learning Abroad, the Department of Political Science, Undergraduate Studies, and the Monteverde Institute. Central to the course is the interdisciplinary and collaborative nature of community-based experiential learning. We will consider and discuss civic leadership, environmental sustainability, community health, and collective impact to better understand people, systems, and places away from home.

title: Creative Disruption for Justice, Equity, and Inclusion
description: Everything around us has been designed and authored—including systems of inequity and injustice. In this course students will critically engage creation narratives (Who benefitted from the design? Who participated in the creative process? Who was harmed by the design?) and examine the foundations of creative and maker-led justice (shared principles, authentic and genuine relationships, longevity and impact, and collaboration and co-creation). Students will explore and identify the potential for positive and negative impact that the creative disciplines can have in the world.

title: Theories of Change for Creative Disruption
description: This course offers an introduction to theories of change for those seeking long-term positive growth within their communities. Students will learn the component parts of a theory of change and explore the strengths/weaknesses of various theories of change (i.e., Positive Deviance, Theory U, Public Narrative, Radical Help, Adaptive Leadership, and social movements including Civil Rights, Women’s Rights, LGBTQI+ Justice). Students will gain an understanding of how methods, concepts, and theories from outside of the creative disciplines are relevant to devising new maker-led strategies for change.

title: Cuba: Complexity, Community, and Change
description: This is a community engaged learning abroad course (CELA) in which we explore the power of community engagement in an international context. We will utilize the history, culture, and civic issues affecting Cuba as a living learning journey. The course is composed of weekly class meetings, readings, discussions, and a week-long community engagement experience in Havana, Cuba. The course is the result of collaborative efforts between The Lowell Bennion Community Service Center, Learning Abroad, the Departments of Political Science, Public Administration, and the Martin Luther King Jr. Center in Havana, Cuba. Central to Cuba: Complexity, Community & Change is the interdisciplinary and collaborative nature of community-based experiential learning. We will consider and discuss civic leadership, community health, systems ecology, and collective impact to better understand how the activities and impacts of traveling and tourism affect quality of life, culture, and community in a historically isolated Latin American country. This course examines the current political, economic, social, and environmental issues in Cuba. Despite the evolving diplomatic relations with the U.S, there remain many misconceptions regarding the internal and external dynamics of Cuba. Through in-class and in-country discussions and activities we will analyze various historical and contemporary perspectives regarding Cuba and the Cuban Revolution.

title: Special Topics in Community Engagement
description: This course allows guest faculty to offer courses on current, pressing, and evolving issues in community engagement across disciplines. Specific content will vary depending on the faculty teaching the course and the Special Topics theme for the year.

title: Community Capstone Methods
description: In this course, students enrolled in the Bennion Scholars program and/or the Community Engagement Certificate will develop and leverage their civic values, skills, habits, and awareness, to meet genuine community needs in their own unique community-engaged capstone project. Students will learn tools and strategies to help build capacity so that individuals, families, and neighborhoods can thrive. Students will engage in mutually beneficial partnerships with faculty members and community organizations to design innovative solutions to issues facing their communities.

title: Creating Inclusive Communities Through Dialogue
description: One of the marks of an educated person should be that the person is capable of having a nonviolent conversation and cooperative relationship with anyone in their local or global community. Every high school in the USA probably has a debate team, but few if any have a dialogue team. Yet dialogue is a process that is much more likely to help students in their future personal and professional lives and is also an effective method of helping humans bridge the differences that divide us and form inclusive local and global communities. Thus, in debate, there is always a “winner” and a “loser”; in dialogue, everyone “wins”. In this class, students will have the opportunity to learn dialogue skills, knowledge, and values. In this largely experiential, “flipped” class, students invite groups from the community to dialogue with us in the classroom. Students are encouraged to bring in groups that are especially challenging, including people who claim, for example, various religious, political, racial, sexual, or cultural identities that conflict with most students in the class. Students have the opportunity to facilitate and participate in these dialogues, as we practice listening for understanding and speaking with respect.

title: Community Engaged Internship
description: Enrolling in this course allows undergraduate students to earn from 1-3 academic credits for successfully completing a community engaged internship or professional experience. In addition to fulfilling the requirements of the internship provider, students will complete bi-weekly assignments. These assignments include reflection about the relevance of the community-based internship experiences, skill-building exercises to help prepare students for the professional world, and guided examination of community-engaged work in the context of community-identified needs and the public purpose of the student’s intended profession.

title: Introduction to Civic Leadership
description: Homelessness, poverty, hunger, illiteracy, air quality, access to healthcare, academic success are some of the issues facing our communities. What are the root causes of these issues? Where do we even begin to address these large and complex challenges?This course has four purposes: 1) introduce students to the theoretical preparation necessary to engage in community work; 2) guide students through learning, discussions, and reflections on the power of collaboration and community movements; 3) facilitate an introspective understanding self, privileges, identities, talents, and power to create positive change in the world; and 4) help students dive into a long-term community engaged learning project that addresses an important community need.The course explores the diverse challenges facing our communities, and investigates the Social Change Model of Leadership. Over the course of the semester, students develop the civic competencies that are needed to make a meaningful contribution to a better world.

title: Systems Thinking Capstone
description: This course will equip students in the Bennion Community Engaged Scholars program with systems thinking skills. Students will learn tools to deeply understand, concisely present, and strategically design responses to social challenges. Students will apply those skills to their respective community-engaged work in order to 1) meaningfully articulate and present hours of engagement and 2) develop a strategic plan to further engage with communities to generate positive social impact.

title: Community Capstone Methods
description: In this course, students enrolled in the Bennion Scholars program and/or the Community Engagement Certificate will develop and leverage their civic values, skills, habits, and awareness, to meet genuine community needs in their own unique community-engaged capstone project. Students will learn tools and strategies to help build capacity so that individuals, families, and neighborhoods can thrive. Students will engage in mutually beneficial partnerships with faculty members and community organizations to design innovative solutions to issues facing their communities.

title: Creative Disruption Capstone Studio I
description: The idea of scenario planning/strategic foresight comes from the perspective that, while the future is not predictable, it is also not predetermined. In this course, students will focus on scenario design for social change. Scenario planning is a discipline for encouraging creative and entrepreneurial thinking and action in contexts of change, complexity, and uncertainty. Students will gain an understanding of the history of scenario planning, ideal situations for using the strategy, the research required to undertake scenario planning, and the process for its implementation. Students will complete a set of scenarios related to creating a more equitable, inclusive, and just future.

title: Community Engagement Practicum
description: Students enrolled in the Community Engagement certificate and/or Bennion Scholars program will participate in a practicum experience to enact a community-engaged project, research, and/or programming. Building upon knowledge and experience gained from prerequisite courses, each student will engage in mutually beneficial partnerships to implement innovative approaches to issues facing their communities. Students taking the 3 credit course commit to working on their project for 50 hours and those taking the 6 credit course commit to working on their project for 100 hours over the semester

title: Creative Disruption Capstone Studio II
description: This course is a continuation of BENN 4845 Creative Justice Capstone Studio I. Working collaboratively alongside community partners and within communities, students will use their prior research to authentically include a diverse range of participants in thoughtful, inclusive, and supportive creative experiences. Throughout the creative process, students will develop, iterate, implement, and reflect on potential solutions to mutually identified community challenges. These projects will involve diverse stakeholders, leverage the complexity of human relationships, critique belief systems, and disrupt power imbalances. By listening to all voices, using generative dialogue, and choosing a human-centric design process of co-creation, students will learn how creatives can be co-leaders of conditions in which just systems can emerge and flourish.

title: Special Topics in Biology K-12 Educators
description: This course is designed to update and enhance K-12 educators' knowledge in biology. Course includes presentations by and discussions with scientists about cutting-edge research, essential background information, as well as experience with hands-on activities and instructional approaches appropriate for classroom use. Course topics change each semester.

title: Biophysical Methods
description: This course will focus on biochemical and biophysical approaches to studying proteins and their functional interactions. Topics covered will include: protein-ligand interactions, cooperativity and allostery, protein folding and design, spectroscopic techniques, analytical ultracentrifugation, calorimetry, biosensors, proteomics approaches, and protein structure prediction.

title: Structural Methods
description: This course provides an integrated approach to the applications of X-ray crystallography and electron microscopy in structural biology. Topics covered include basic theory and the application of methods of structure determination.

title: Regulation of Metabolism
description: This half-semester course will begin with a review of carbohydrate and lipid metabolic pathways, with an emphasis on an integrated understanding the pathways and what is known about their regulation.  The course will progress to an in-depth analysis of current research in specific areas of nutritional sensing and metabolic regulation.

title: Regulation of Metabolism (for MD students)
description: This courses meets together with Bio C 6600, and is the specific section for medical students. The focus is on the regulation of sugar and fat metabolism in eukaryotes, with an emphasis on mammals. The course will begin with a review of carbohydrate and lipid metabolic pathways, particularly pathway integration and regulation. The middle portion of the course is an exposure to the breadth of metabolism research occurring here at University of Utah Health Sciences. The course ends with students teaching a metabolism topic of their own choosing to the class.

title: Cell Biology Selective
description: Cell biology was redesigned in 2019 with a decreased emphasis on didactic lectures and a stronger focus on teaching students how to read and evaluate primary literature. The course consists of primary research articles within the field, and each class will discuss one paper. Students will be expected to read/watch background material posted on Canvas prior to each class. Objectives are as follows:1. To effectively assess data in published literature. To be able to answer:a. What are the questions the authors seek to answer?b. What approaches did the authors use to answer the questions? Why did they use those approaches?c. Did the presented data answer the questions? How convinced are you?d. If you had access to unlimited resources, how would you follow up on this work? What questions would you ask, and how would you answer them?2. To be able to articulate scientific knowledge both verbally and in written format3. To gain a basic understanding of selected topics in cell biology

title: Graduate Seminar
description: Students and faculty report on current papers from biochemical literature.

title: Biochemical Techniques
description: Short tutorial courses for individual instruction in specialized techniques.

title: Topics in Biochemistry
description: Seminar: Student and faculty discussion of advanced-level topics not covered in formal courses.

title: Genetic Therapies
description: This intensive, 2-week, 2-credit elective course is designed to introduce advanced medical and graduate students to issues that will be relevant to research and practice in an era of molecular medicine. Emerging therapies based on genetic abnormalities are promising, but also frequently controversial. The course will begin with early efforts at gene therapy and attendant ethical considerations. It will cover contemporary methods for gene manipulation and recent clinical experience with genetic therapies. Human germline modification will be covered, as will current efforts at regulation and societal issues, including justice and access. Because of its relevance to human health, uses of genetic manipulation in food organisms will also be discussed. Relevant readings, largely from the primary literature, will be assigned for each session. In the first week, class time will consist of presentations by the faculty, discussions of the lecture material and the assigned readings, and broader discussion of issues raised by these exposures. In the second week, student presentations will replace a portion of the lecture time, the extent depending on the number of students enrolled. The student presentations will be on literature-based topics chosen by the students themselves in consultation with the faculty.
